title: Introduction to OOP
learningObjectives: Define the four OOP pillars and map Java language features to each
Implement encapsulation with private fields, constructors, and controlled mutators
Use inheritance and @Override responsibly with Liskov-safe behaviour
Program to supertype references to demonstrate polymorphic dispatch
Chain constructors with this() and initialise superclass state with super()
Sketch abstract types and interfaces as abstraction boundaries for collaborators

sections.title: Why classes, encapsulation, and polymorphism anchor production Java
sections.content:
Object-oriented programming is how Java teams model domains: entities with hidden state, constructors that establish invariants, and polymorphic behaviour selected at runtime. When classes leak internal representation or subclasses break superclass contracts, production incidents look like inconsistent business rules, not syntax errors. Interviewers probe OOP because it predicts whether you can design APIs that stay safe as requirements evolve.

Encapsulation is the daily practice of **private** fields with controlled **getters** and **mutators** that validate input. Inheritance expresses **is-a** relationships when behaviour truly generalises; composition often ages better when only behaviour is shared. Polymorphism lets you program to **supertype** references while concrete subclasses supply specialised `area()` or `process()` implementations. Abstraction hides machinery behind interfaces and abstract classes so callers depend on stable contracts.

Constructors initialise object graphs: default constructors, parameterised constructors, and **`this()`** chains that delegate to a single source of truth. Forgetting to initialise **final** fields, calling **`this()`** or **`super()``** twice, or placing statements before them causes compile errors that juniors misread as “Java is picky” instead of “object lifecycle has a defined order.”

The four pillars are not slogans—they are review criteria. **Encapsulation** fails when `public` fields allow negative balances. **Inheritance** fails when `Square` extends `Rectangle` and violates Liskov expectations. **Polymorphism** fails when `instanceof` cascades replace real abstractions. **Abstraction** fails when every service reaches into concrete implementations of payment gateways instead of narrow interfaces.

Day seven’s assignment chains pillars, constructor chaining, a **`BankAccount`**-style invariant story, and a **`Shape` / `Circle` / `Rectangle`** hierarchy with overridden **`area()`**. Those exercises mirror how you justify design choices in code review: which field is **private**, which method is **`final`**, and where **`@Override`** documents intent.

During **on-call**, OOP design shows up when serializers bypass getters, frameworks reflect private fields, or two microservices embed different assumptions about the same DTO. The debugging skill is tracing **object identity** and **class loader** boundaries, then tightening encapsulation or adding explicit DTO mappers. This day trains you to speak about classes the way staff engineers do: start with invariants, show how constructors enforce them, explain dynamic dispatch on one example, and end with a test that would catch a broken override. That cadence maps cleanly to Spring beans, domain entities, and UI models alike. When you mentor juniors, connect OOP to observability: immutable value objects for events, rich domain methods instead of anemic data bags, and polymorphic handlers behind interfaces so you can swap implementations without rewriting controllers. Those habits reduce regression rates more than any single keyword trick. `@Override` is not decoration—it is a compile-time guard that catches signature drift when superclasses rename methods. `final` on classes or methods documents intent: extension points are deliberate, not accidental. `abstract` types force implementors to complete behaviour instead of shipping hollow shells that throw at runtime. In security-sensitive areas, encapsulation is your first firewall: never expose mutable internal collections without wrapping, never return references to defensive copies you still mutate elsewhere, and validate constructor arguments so illegal objects cannot exist. Interviewers listen for that language because it predicts production hygiene beyond textbook UML. Polymorphic collections—`List<Shape>` holding `Circle` and `Rectangle`—are where dynamic dispatch becomes tangible. Iteration calls the most specific `area()` without `instanceof` ladders, keeping open-closed principles within reach. When performance demands devirtualisation, HotSpot may inline hot monomorphic calls, but your design should still read clearly to humans first. On-call you will thank past-you for small, cohesive classes: stack traces name exact types, logging can include concise `toString()` output, and feature flags can target implementations behind interfaces instead of scattering conditionals across packages. Carry that mindset into every Phase 1 exercise: the goal is not bigger classes, but sharper boundaries and clearer tests.

sections.title: Theory and Internals — pillars, constructors, inheritance, and dynamic dispatch
sections.content:
### Plain-language overview

**Classes** are blueprints; **objects** are concrete instances living on the heap with their own field values. **Methods** implement behaviour and may read or mutate state according to access rules. **Object-oriented programming** in Java emphasises **encapsulation** (hide fields, expose operations), **inheritance** (reuse and specialise), **polymorphism** (substitute subtypes where supertypes are expected), and **abstraction** (depend on interfaces and abstract APIs). Interviewers expect you to connect these pillars to real API design, not UML trivia.

### 1. Encapsulation with access modifiers

`private` fields prevent external mutation; `public` methods define the contract. Package-private and `protected` widen visibility deliberately. **Interview angle:** Public mutable fields break invariants—`BankAccount` balances should not be tweakable from anywhere.

### 2. Constructors and object initialisation

Constructors run after super constructors, initialise `final` fields, and may chain with `this(...)` or `super(...)`. Those calls must be the **first** statement. **Interview angle:** Validate arguments early—illegal objects should not exist.

### 3. `this` reference

`this` refers to the current instance; useful for disambiguating parameters from fields (`this.balance = balance`). **Interview angle:** `static` methods lack `this`.

### 4. Inheritance and `extends`

Subclass inherits non-private members and may **override** instance methods. Constructors are not inherited; subclass must invoke a superclass constructor. **Interview angle:** Prefer composition when behaviour sharing is not a true **is-a** relationship.

### 5. Method overriding and `@Override`

Same signature in subclass replaces superclass implementation; dynamic dispatch chooses the runtime type. `@Override` catches typos at compile time. **Interview angle:** Covariant return types allowed; parameter lists must match (with generics erasure caveats).

### 6. `super` calls

`super.method()` calls superclass version; needed when subclass overrides but still wants partial reuse. **Interview angle:** Constructor `super(args)` wires parent state first.

### 7. Polymorphism

A `Shape s = new Circle();` reference invokes `Circle`’s `area()` at runtime. **Interview angle:** Avoid `instanceof` ladders when polymorphism or visitor patterns clarify intent.

### 8. Abstract classes and interfaces

`abstract` classes may carry state and partial implementation; interfaces define contracts (Java 8+ allow `default` methods). **Interview angle:** Use interfaces for capabilities; abstract classes for shared skeletal code.

### 9. `final` modifiers

`final` class cannot be extended; `final` method cannot be overridden; `final` field assigns once. **Interview angle:** Documents immutability or closed extension points.

### 10. Object identity versus equality

`==` compares references; `equals` defines logical equality—often overridden with `hashCode` consistency. **Interview angle:** Value objects should implement both carefully.

### 11. `toString`, `equals`, `hashCode`

IDE-generated or hand-written triad underpins collections and logging. **Interview angle:** Breaking `equals/hashCode` contracts corrupts `HashMap`.

### 12. Composition

Hold collaborators as fields instead of subclassing for reuse. **Interview angle:** Favour composition when only behaviour overlaps, not identity.

### 13. Immutability pattern

`final` fields + no mutators + defensive copies produce immutable objects useful for events and cache keys. **Interview angle:** Contrast with beans exposing setters everywhere.

### 14. Constructors versus factories

Static factory methods can name intents (`of`, `from`) and cache instances. **Interview angle:** Constructors cannot be async; factories hide complexity.

### 15. Assignment tie-in

Day 7 covers pillars, constructor chaining, `BankAccount`-style encapsulation, and `Shape` hierarchy with `area()` overrides. **Interview angle:** Map each requirement to a pillar.

### 16. 60-second interview story

Encapsulate with `private` fields and validated methods. Use inheritance for true specialisation; override with `@Override` and respect contracts. Program to supertypes for polymorphism. Abstract details behind interfaces. Constructors enforce invariants and may chain. `final` documents non-extension. Test subclasses for Liskov-safe behaviour.

### 17. Satyverse drill

Sketch `Box()` chaining to `Box(1,1,1)`. List `private` fields for `BankAccount`. Write `Shape` references pointing at `Circle` and `Rectangle` in one list.

### 18. Pitfall rehearsal table

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---------|--------------|-----|
| `NullPointerException` in ctor | `super` ordering / leaking `this` | reorder init |
| Wrong method at runtime | hiding static vs overriding instance | learn dispatch rules |
| Broken `HashSet` | bad `equals/hashCode` | regenerate consistently |

### 19. Interview angle — closing

End OOP answers with **invariants** and **tests** that prove subclasses cannot break expectations.

### 20. Pillars quick map

| Pillar | Java mechanism |
|--------|----------------|
| Encapsulation | `private` + accessors |
| Inheritance | `extends` |
| Polymorphism | `@Override` + dynamic dispatch |
| Abstraction | `interface` / `abstract` |

### OOP discipline layer 1

Classes bundle state and behaviour; encapsulation hides representation so invariants survive refactors. When polymorphic dispatch that is accidentally bypassed by instanceof chains or reflection hacks, reviewers demand stricter accessors, composition over deep hierarchies, or sealed patterns documented in ADRs. Polymorphism relies on consistent contracts from abstract supertypes or interfaces. **Interview angle:** Name one invariant your constructor enforces and one test that would fail if a subclass broke Liskov expectations.

sections.title: Basic — four pillars, access modifiers, constructor and @Override hints
sections.output:
----------------------------------------
=== Four pillars (one line each) ===
Encapsulation -> hide fields, expose safe operations
Inheritance -> specialised is-a reuse via extends
Polymorphism -> one reference, many runtime behaviours
Abstraction -> depend on interfaces/abstract contracts
=== Class vs object ===
class Shape -> blueprint; new Circle() -> concrete instance
=== Access modifiers ===
private -> class only; package -> same package; protected -> package+subclasses; public -> all
=== Constructor hint ===
this() and super() must be first statement; chain to avoid duplication
=== @Override hint ===
Annotate overrides to catch signature typos at compile time
=== equals/hashCode reminder ===
Always update both together before using HashMap keys
=== composition cue ===
Prefer delegating to collaborators over deep extends trees
=== toString cue ===
Redact secrets but keep type + id for logs
done
=== OOP team guardrail quick checks ===
Contract review  | Check inheritance and composition boundaries before release
Heap profile     | Validate object churn in hot polymorphic paths with jcmd
On-call playbook | Capture jstack + failing class hierarchy assumptions first

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Exposing `public` mutable fields for domain entities — symptom is negative balances or illegal state bypassing methods; detect in review; fix with `private` fields and validated mutators.
Using inheritance for code reuse only — symptom is fragile hierarchies (`Square`/`Rectangle` Liskov issues); detect via failing substitutability tests; fix with composition or redesign contracts.
Forgetting `@Override` and silently adding overloads — symptom is superclass method never called; detect with unused code warnings; fix annotate and match signatures exactly.
Breaking `equals` without matching `hashCode` — symptom is lost `HashMap` entries; detect with unit tests on collections; fix regenerate pair consistently.
Calling overridable methods from constructors — symptom is observing subclass fields before they initialise; detect with NPE in logs; fix make methods `final` or use static helpers.
Leaking `this` during construction to other threads — symptom is races on partially built objects; detect with concurrency tests; fix avoid publishing early.
`instanceof` ladders instead of polymorphism — symptom is high churn when adding types; detect in static analysis; fix introduce interface or visitor.
Marking everything `final` on classes during prototyping without plan — symptom is untestable mocks in legacy code; detect in test suites; fix carve extension points deliberately.

sections.title: Exercise — Day 7 assignment: pillars, constructor chaining, BankAccount-style rules, Shape hierarchy
sections.problem:
Implement `arch.day7.Day7Exercise` covering **assignments_phase1.json** Day 7 themes (pillars explanation, constructor chaining, encapsulated account, `Shape`/`Circle`/`Rectangle`).

Requirements:
1. Print **four** numbered lines naming the **four OOP pillars** with one-sentence definitions each (encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction).
2. Add a static nested `Box` class with `Box()` chaining to `Box(1,1,1)` and a `toString()`; `main` must print `new Box()` showing `1x1x1`.
3. Add a static nested `BankAccount` with **private** `double balance`, constructor rejecting negative starting balance (throw `IllegalArgumentException` with a fixed message `bad initial`), `deposit(double)` rejecting non-positive amounts, `withdraw(double)` returning `false` when funds insufficient else mutates and returns `true`, and `getBalance()`. In `main`, create an account with `100.0`, deposit `25.0`, attempt withdraw `200.0`, then withdraw `50.0`, printing balance after each step as **exactly** `balance=VALUE` using the instance you created.
4. Add `Shape` with `String color` field and `area()` returning `0.0` by default, `Circle` with radius field overriding `area()` using `pi = 3.141592653589793`, `Rectangle` with width/height overriding `area()`. Print `area` for a `Circle("red",3)` and `Rectangle("blue",4,5)` each on its own line prefixed `shape-area:`.
5. Include a comment tying **polymorphism** to storing both shapes behind a `Shape` reference in a tiny array loop (you may implement the loop printing both areas with the same code path).
hints: Keep `BankAccount` invariants centralised—never set `balance` without validation.
Use `@Override` on `Circle`/`Rectangle` `area` to catch signature drift early.
Constructor chaining: `this(1,1,1)` must be the first statement in `Box()`.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: Name the four pillars of OOP and map each to a Java mechanism.
conceptual.answer: For **Name the four pillars of OOP and map each to a Java mechanism.** in the context of **Introduction to OOP**, start from JVM internals instead of only syntax. The JVM executes operations through concrete bytecode instructions and memory models, so data representation and control decisions directly affect runtime behavior. In production, weak conceptual understanding becomes incident fuel: hidden allocations, poor branch behavior, or boundary errors increase latency, retry pressure, and GC churn. Diagnose with real tooling: use `jcmd <pid> GC.heap_info` for memory posture, `jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000 10` for GC pressure trends, and `javap -c` to inspect what bytecode is actually emitted for critical logic. Version awareness matters too: Java 8/11/17/21 differ in defaults and runtime optimizations, so migration testing must validate both correctness and performance. Keep the explanation fresher-friendly by pairing internal mechanism with one simple concrete example, but keep staff-level depth by connecting every concept to an operational consequence and an observable metric.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how do **OOP** choices affect **production** incidents?
followUps.answer: For pillars, you see leaks of internal state, broken equals/hashCode in caches, and subclasses that violate superclass expectations. You tighten encapsulation, add regression tests for substitutability, and document invariants in constructors. You verify with canaries and structured logs that reference interface types, not concrete hacks.

followUps.question: What **trap** might follow your answer?
followUps.answer: Interviewers may ask about reflection bypassing `private`, or serialization breaking singletons. You respond with defensive copies, `SecurityManager` history awareness, and immutable DTOs for APIs. You close with one test that would catch a bad override or illegal construction path.

seniorScenario.question: DTOs with public fields shipped to partners—risk?
seniorScenario.answer:
**Immediate response:** I stabilize impact first for the Introduction to OOP path by checking user-facing error rate, p95/p99 latency, and rollback options before touching code. I collect runtime evidence with `jcmd <pid> VM.version`, `jcmd <pid> VM.flags`, `jstack <pid>`, and a short `jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000 20` sample so decisions are data-driven within the first five minutes.

**Root cause:** For **DTOs with public fields shipped to partners—risk?**, the usual root cause is mismatch between intended Java behavior and actual JVM execution characteristics (allocation shape, bytecode branch paths, or boundary assumptions). Under realistic load, this mismatch manifests as exception spikes, throughput collapse, or GC pause amplification that may not appear in narrow local tests.

**Fix:** I apply a narrow correction with explicit guardrails: harden edge conditions, remove avoidable boxing/copying in hot paths, and add deterministic fallback logic where needed. I verify with targeted tests, canary rollout metrics, `javap -c` spot checks for emitted bytecode, and post-deploy SLO comparisons to confirm the change improves behavior without side effects.

**Prevention:** I codify prevention in process and architecture: add checklist rules for this topic in code review, enforce boundary/performance tests in CI, and track dashboards that correlate allocation rate, exception counts, and latency. I also document Java 8/11/17/21 migration notes so future upgrades treat runtime behavior as a first-class validation target, not just compile success.

wrongAnswers: `private` methods participate in dynamic polymorphic overriding exactly like instance methods.
You can chain two `this()` calls as long as they are both somewhere in the constructor.
`equals` may be overridden without touching `hashCode` because the JVM auto-syncs them.
Static methods override instance methods when signatures match in a subclass.
Abstract classes can be instantiated with `new` if they have only default constructors.
`protected` fields are safe to expose because only subclasses outside the package can see them.
Polymorphism means the compiler always picks subclass methods using variable declared type.
Composition always requires inheritance to share code between unrelated classes.

options.B: Choose JVM-aware behavior with observability and safe rollout
options.C: Ignore edge cases and rely on retries
options.D: Assume all Java versions behave identically

sections.title: Cheatsheet — classes, objects, and OOP pillars
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| Encapsulation | private fields + methods | "Never let outsiders break invariants." |
| Inheritance | extends for true is-a | "Prefer composition when behaviour only overlaps." |
| Polymorphism | override + supertype refs | "Let virtual calls replace instanceof ladders." |
| Abstraction | interfaces / abstract | "Depend on contracts, hide creation." |
| Constructor chain | this()/super() first | "Delegate defaults to one real ctor." |
| @Override | compile guard | "Catches accidental overloads." |
| final class | no subclasses | "Use when design is closed." |
| final method | no override | "Pin hot spots or security hooks." |
| abstract class | partial template | "Share code + force overrides." |
| interface | capability contract | "Multiple types, one API surface." |
| equals/hashCode | paired | "Broken pair corrupts HashMap." |
| Liskov | substitutable subtypes | "Subclass must honour superclass promises." |
| Protected | package + kids | "Not a public escape hatch." |
| static | belongs to class | "No this; not polymorphic." |
| toString | debuggability | "Log friendly without leaking secrets." |
